$comment: Feature metadata for terminfo.dev probes. Keyed by probe dot-path ID.

sgr.bold.name: Bold (SGR 1)
sgr.bold.body: SGR 1 activates bold text rendering. The sequence is ESC [ 1 m. Bold is one of the original VT100 text attributes and is universally supported across terminals. Historically, some terminals render SGR 1 as increased intensity (brighter color) rather than heavier font weight — and some do both. When bold is active alongside one of the standard foreground colors (SGR 30-37), certain terminals will substitute the corresponding bright color (SGR 90-97). This means bold red may appear as bright red rather than bold red, depending on the terminal and its configuration. Bold is reset by SGR 0 (reset all) or SGR 22 (reset bold/dim). Note that SGR 22 resets both bold and faint — there is no way to reset one without the other.
sgr.bold.probe: Send \x1b[1mX, verify the cell at (0,0) has bold=true. Confirms the SGR 1 attribute is parsed and applied to the character cell.

sgr.faint.name: Faint/dim (SGR 2)
sgr.faint.body: SGR 2 renders text with reduced intensity (dimmed). The sequence is ESC [ 2 m. Commonly used for secondary or de-emphasized text in TUI applications — status bars, disabled items, or metadata. Faint is reset by SGR 22, which also resets bold (SGR 1). There is no separate reset code for faint alone. Most modern terminals support faint rendering, typically by reducing the alpha or brightness of the foreground color.
sgr.faint.probe: Send \x1b[2mX, verify the cell at (0,0) has dim=true. Confirms SGR 2 (faint) is parsed and stored as a distinct attribute.

sgr.italic.name: Italic (SGR 3)
sgr.italic.body: SGR 3 renders text in italic style. The sequence is ESC [ 3 m. Reset with SGR 23. Italic requires the terminal font to have an italic variant — monospace fonts without italics typically render it as normal text or with a slight color change. Italic was not supported on early hardware terminals and was historically rendered as inverse video on some systems. Modern terminals with proper font support render true italics, commonly used for comments, emphasis, and documentation in TUI applications and syntax-highlighted output.
sgr.italic.probe: Send \x1b[3mX, verify the cell at (0,0) has italic=true.

sgr.underline.single.name: Underline (SGR 4)
sgr.underline.single.body: SGR 4 draws a single underline beneath text. The sequence is ESC [ 4 m. One of the original VT100 attributes, universally supported across terminals. Reset with SGR 24. Underline was one of the original hardware-friendly emphasis attributes: it worked on monochrome terminals where color was unavailable. Modern terminals still treat SGR 4 as the compatibility floor for diagnostics, links, and emphasis. Richer underline styles build on top of this convention, but SGR 4 remains the fallback every app can expect.
sgr.underline.single.probe: Send \x1b[4mX, verify the cell has a truthy underline attribute.

sgr.underline.double.name: Double underline (SGR 21)
sgr.underline.double.body: SGR 21 draws a double underline beneath text. The sequence is ESC [ 21 m. Also accessible via the Kitty sub-parameter syntax as ESC [ 4:2 m. Reset with SGR 24. Support varies — some older terminals treat SGR 21 as bold-off rather than double underline.
sgr.underline.double.probe: Send \x1b[21mX, verify underline === "double". Tests whether SGR 21 is interpreted as double underline rather than bold-off.

sgr.underline.curly.name: Curly underline (SGR 4:3)
sgr.underline.curly.body: SGR 4:3 draws a wavy/curly underline beneath text, using the ECMA-48 colon sub-parameter syntax: ESC [ 4:3 m. This underline style was introduced by Kitty and is commonly used by editors and IDEs to indicate spelling errors, lint warnings, or diagnostic information — similar to the red squiggly underline in graphical editors. Combine with SGR 58 (underline color) to set the underline color independently of the text color, e.g., red curly underline under normally-colored text for errors. Adopted by Ghostty, WezTerm, foot, and other modern terminals. Reset with SGR 24.

sgr.underline.dotted.name: Dotted underline (SGR 4:4)
sgr.underline.dotted.body: SGR 4:4 draws a dotted underline beneath text: ESC [ 4:4 m. Part of the Kitty extended underline styles. Can be combined with SGR 58 for colored underlines. Reset with SGR 24. Dotted underline is part of the modern styled-underline family popularized by Kitty and adopted by other terminals. Editors use it for low-severity diagnostics, spelling hints, and subtle annotations where a full solid underline would be too strong. Terminals that do not recognize the sub-parameter should ignore it or fall back gracefully.

sgr.underline.dashed.name: Dashed underline (SGR 4:5)
sgr.underline.dashed.body: SGR 4:5 draws a dashed underline beneath text: ESC [ 4:5 m. Part of the Kitty extended underline styles. Can be combined with SGR 58 for colored underlines. Reset with SGR 24. Dashed underline fills the gap between a normal underline and the more attention-grabbing curly diagnostic underline. It uses ECMA-48 sub-parameter syntax while relying on a terminal extension for meaning. Applications should still pair it with SGR 24, because not every terminal tracks styled underline variants separately.

sgr.underline.color.name: Underline color (SGR 58)
sgr.underline.color.body: SGR 58 sets the underline color independently of the text foreground color. The syntax mirrors SGR 38 (foreground): ESC [ 58:2::R:G:B m for truecolor, ESC [ 58:5:N m for 256-color. Reset the underline color to default with SGR 59. This is particularly useful with curly underlines (SGR 4:3) for diagnostic indicators: red underline for errors, yellow for warnings, blue for hints — all while keeping the text color unchanged. Introduced by Kitty and adopted by other modern terminals.
sgr.underline.color.probe: Send \x1b[4m\x1b[58;2;255;0;128mX, verify the cell has underline active and underlineColor matches RGB(255, 0, 128).

sgr.underline-color-indexed.name: Underline color, indexed (SGR 58;5)
sgr.underline-color-indexed.body: SGR 58;5;N sets the underline color to one of the 256 indexed palette colors, mirroring the SGR 38;5 syntax used for foreground color. The sequence is ESC [ 58 ; 5 ; N m, where N is a palette index (0-15 system colors, 16-231 6×6×6 RGB cube, 232-255 grayscale ramp). The colon-separated form ESC [ 58 : 5 : N m is also accepted by terminals that support ECMA-48 sub-parameter syntax. Indexed underline color is widely supported on terminals that implement the broader Kitty extended underline protocol: Kitty, WezTerm, foot, Ghostty, VTE-based terminals (GNOME Terminal, Tilix), mintty, and iTerm2. Combine with curly underlines (SGR 4:3) to render diagnostic indicators in palette colors that match the user's theme. Reset the underline color to default (typically the foreground color) with SGR 59.
sgr.underline-color-indexed.probe: Send \x1b[4m\x1b[58;5;5mX, verify the cell at (0,0) has underline active. If the backend tracks underline color per cell, also verify underlineColor matches palette index 5 (magenta).

sgr.underline-color-rgb.name: Underline color, truecolor (SGR 58;2)
sgr.underline-color-rgb.body: SGR 58;2;R;G;B sets the underline color to a 24-bit truecolor value, mirroring the SGR 38;2 syntax used for foreground color. The sequence is ESC [ 58 ; 2 ; R ; G ; B m, where R, G, and B are 0-255. The ECMA-48 colon-separated form ESC [ 58 : 2 : : R : G : B m (with an empty colorspace ID slot) is also accepted by terminals that follow the strict spec. Truecolor underlines were originally introduced by Kitty and have since been adopted by every major modern terminal: WezTerm, foot, Ghostty, Alacritty, VTE (GNOME Terminal, Tilix), iTerm2, mintty, Konsole, and Windows Terminal. The underline color is independent of the text foreground color, so applications can render red squiggly underlines for errors and yellow squigglies for warnings while keeping the surrounding text in its normal color. Reset the underline color to default (typically matching the foreground) with SGR 59.
sgr.underline-color-rgb.probe: Send \x1b[4m\x1b[58;2;255;0;128mX, verify the cell at (0,0) has underline active. If the backend tracks underline color per cell, also verify underlineColor matches RGB(255, 0, 128).

sgr.underline-color-reset.name: Reset underline color (SGR 59)
sgr.underline-color-reset.body: SGR 59 resets the underline color to its default value, which typically matches the current text foreground color. The sequence is ESC [ 59 m. This is the companion reset for SGR 58 (set underline color), analogous to how SGR 39 resets the foreground color set by SGR 38. Using SGR 59 allows applications to clear an explicit underline color without resetting other attributes — the underline style itself (single, double, curly, dotted, dashed) and any other SGR state remain in effect. To remove the underline entirely, use SGR 24 instead. SGR 59 is supported wherever SGR 58 is supported: Kitty, WezTerm, foot, Ghostty, Alacritty, VTE-based terminals, iTerm2, mintty, Konsole, and Windows Terminal.
sgr.underline-color-reset.probe: Send \x1b[4m\x1b[58;2;255;0;128mX\x1b[59mY, verify the cell at (0,0) has the colored underline and the cell at (0,1) has underline active but no explicit underlineColor (or a null/default value).

sgr.blink.name: Blink (SGR 5)
sgr.blink.body: SGR 5 causes text to blink at a steady rate. The sequence is ESC [ 5 m. Many modern terminals intentionally do not implement blinking, as it is considered distracting and an accessibility concern. Terminals that do support it typically allow the user to disable it in preferences.

sgr.inverse.name: Inverse video (SGR 7)
sgr.inverse.body: SGR 7 swaps the foreground and background colors of the text. The sequence is ESC [ 7 m. Universally supported. Commonly used for selection highlighting, cursor rendering, and visual emphasis in TUI applications. Reset with SGR 27. Inverse video is the durable selection primitive of terminal UI. It works in monochrome, low-color, and truecolor environments because it depends on swapping roles rather than choosing a new RGB value. Modern renderers still use it for cursors and selections, but must combine it correctly with explicit foreground/background colors.

sgr.hidden.name: Hidden/invisible (SGR 8)
sgr.hidden.body: SGR 8 renders text invisible by setting the foreground color to match the background. The sequence is ESC [ 8 m. The text is still present and selectable — it is hidden visually but not removed from the terminal buffer. Reset with SGR 28. Hidden text is a visual attribute, not a security boundary. The bytes remain in scrollback and are often selectable or copyable. It is useful for simple reveal/hide effects and legacy password prompts, but applications handling secrets should avoid writing sensitive content to the terminal at all.

sgr.strikethrough.name: Strikethrough (SGR 9)
sgr.strikethrough.body: SGR 9 draws a horizontal line through the middle of the text. The sequence is ESC [ 9 m. Used in TUI applications to indicate deleted, deprecated, or completed items. Reset with SGR 29. Widely supported in modern terminals. Strikethrough is a later text-decoration attribute compared with bold, underline, and inverse video, but modern TUIs use it heavily for completed tasks, deleted text, and diff views. The important compatibility point is cleanup: use SGR 29 rather than SGR 0 when only the strike attribute should end.

sgr.overline.name: Overline (SGR 53)
sgr.overline.body: SGR 53 draws a line above the text, the mirror image of underline. The sequence is ESC [ 53 m. Reset with SGR 55. Overline is one of the lesser-used ECMA-48 attributes and was adopted by modern terminals relatively recently. It can be useful for horizontal rules, header decoration, or combined with underline for a boxed text effect.
sgr.overline.probe: Send \x1b[53mX, check for overline property on the cell.

sgr.fg.standard.name: Standard fg colors (SGR 30-37)
sgr.fg.standard.body: SGR 30-37 set the foreground text color to one of 8 standard colors: black (30), red (31), green (32), yellow (33), blue (34), magenta (35), cyan (36), white (37). Universally supported. The actual displayed color depends on the terminal's color scheme — "red" in one theme may look very different from "red" in another.
sgr.fg.standard.probe: Send \x1b[31mX (red), verify the cell's foreground color has a red channel > 100.

sgr.bg.standard.name: Standard bg colors (SGR 40-47)
sgr.bg.standard.body: SGR 40-47 set the background color to one of 8 standard colors: black (40), red (41), green (42), yellow (43), blue (44), magenta (45), cyan (46), white (47). Universally supported. As with foreground colors, the actual RGB values depend on the terminal's configured color scheme.
sgr.bg.standard.probe: Send \x1b[42mX (green bg), verify the cell's background color has a green channel > 100.

sgr.fg.bright.name: Bright fg colors (SGR 90-97)
sgr.fg.bright.body: SGR 90-97 set the foreground to one of 8 bright (high-intensity) colors, corresponding to the standard colors at higher intensity. Originally an xterm extension, now universally supported. These provide 8 additional named colors beyond SGR 30-37 without requiring 256-color or truecolor support.
sgr.fg.bright.probe: Send \x1b[91mX (bright red), verify the foreground red channel > 150.

sgr.bg.bright.name: Bright bg colors (SGR 100-107)
sgr.bg.bright.body:
SGR 100-107 set the background to one of 8 bright (high-intensity) colors. The bright background counterpart to SGR 90-97 bright foreground colors. Originally an xterm extension, now universally supported.

Bright background colors are the high-intensity half of the 16-color ANSI palette. They began as terminal-emulator extensions rather than original VT100 hardware behavior, but are now expected by color libraries and themes. The exact RGB values are theme-defined, so applications should treat them as named slots, not fixed colors.
sgr.bg.bright.probe: Send \x1b[102mX (bright green bg), verify the background green channel > 150.

sgr.fg.default.name: Default fg color (SGR 39)
sgr.fg.default.body: SGR 39 resets the foreground color to the terminal's default, without affecting other attributes. The sequence is ESC [ 39 m. This is more targeted than SGR 0, which resets all attributes. Useful when you want to clear a color change while preserving bold, underline, or other active attributes.
sgr.fg.default.probe: Send \x1b[31mX\x1b[39mY, verify the second cell's foreground is reset to default (null).

sgr.bg.default.name: Default bg color (SGR 49)
sgr.bg.default.body: SGR 49 resets the background color to the terminal's default, without affecting other attributes. The sequence is ESC [ 49 m. The counterpart of SGR 39 (default foreground). Default-color resets let applications return to the user's theme instead of assuming black, white, or any particular palette entry. This is especially important for translucent terminals, dark/light theme switching, and accessibility themes. Use SGR 49 for background cleanup when foreground color and other attributes should remain active.
sgr.bg.default.probe: Send \x1b[42mX\x1b[49mY, verify the second cell's background is reset to default (null).

sgr.fg.256.name: 256-color fg (SGR 38;5)
sgr.fg.256.body: SGR 38;5;N sets the foreground to one of 256 colors. The sequence is ESC [ 38;5;N m where N is 0-255. Colors 0-7 are the standard colors, 8-15 are bright, 16-231 are a 6x6x6 color cube, and 232-255 are a grayscale ramp. This palette provides a reasonable range of colors that works across most modern terminals without requiring truecolor support.
sgr.fg.256.probe: Send \x1b[38;5;196mX (256-color red), verify the foreground red channel > 200.

sgr.bg.256.name: 256-color bg (SGR 48;5)
sgr.bg.256.body: SGR 48;5;N sets the background to one of 256 colors. The sequence is ESC [ 48;5;N m. Same palette as SGR 38;5 (256-color foreground): 0-15 standard/bright, 16-231 color cube, 232-255 grayscale. The 256-color palette is the bridge between named ANSI colors and truecolor. It combines 16 theme colors, a 6x6x6 RGB cube, and a grayscale ramp. Background 256-color support is still important when truecolor is unavailable or disabled, especially over older SSH, tmux, or terminfo configurations.
sgr.bg.256.probe: Send \x1b[48;5;21mX (256-color blue), verify the background blue channel > 100.

sgr.fg.truecolor.name: 24-bit fg (SGR 38;2)
sgr.fg.truecolor.body: SGR 38;2;R;G;B sets the foreground to a 24-bit RGB color. The sequence is ESC [ 38;2;R;G;B m where R, G, B are 0-255. There is also a colon-separated variant ESC [ 38:2::R:G:B m per ITU T.416, but the semicolon format has wider compatibility. This gives applications access to 16.7 million colors. Applications can check for truecolor support via COLORTERM=truecolor or COLORTERM=24bit in the environment.
sgr.fg.truecolor.probe: Send \x1b[38;2;255;128;0mX, verify the foreground color matches RGB(255, 128, 0) exactly.

sgr.bg.truecolor.name: 24-bit bg (SGR 48;2)
sgr.bg.truecolor.body: SGR 48;2;R;G;B sets the background to a 24-bit RGB color. The sequence is ESC [ 48;2;R;G;B m. Same syntax variants and compatibility notes as SGR 38;2 (24-bit foreground). The background form follows the same direct-color convention as SGR 38;2 for foregrounds. In practice, the semicolon form is what most terminals and applications emit, while the colon-separated ISO/ITU-style form is less consistently supported. Background truecolor is what makes theme previews, color pickers, and high-fidelity terminal screenshots possible.
sgr.bg.truecolor.probe: Send \x1b[48;2;0;255;128mX, verify the background color matches RGB(0, 255, 128) exactly.

sgr.selective-reset.bold.name: Reset bold/dim (SGR 22)
sgr.selective-reset.bold.body: SGR 22 resets both bold (SGR 1) and faint/dim (SGR 2) attributes. The sequence is ESC [ 22 m. There is no way to reset bold without also resetting faint, or vice versa — they share this single reset code. This paired reset is one of the awkward corners of SGR. ECMA-48 uses SGR 22 for both bold and faint, so applications cannot clear only one of those attributes portably. Emulators should preserve that shared reset behavior even if their internal style model stores bold and dim separately.
sgr.selective-reset.bold.probe: Send \x1b[1mX\x1b[22mY, verify the second cell has bold=false and dim=false.

sgr.selective-reset.underline.name: Reset underline (SGR 24)
sgr.selective-reset.underline.body: SGR 24 resets all underline styles — single, double, curly, dotted, and dashed. The sequence is ESC [ 24 m. Targeted resets are important for nested styling: an application can clear an underline without also discarding color, bold, inverse video, or other active SGR attributes. Modern terminals map SGR 24 across both classic ECMA-48 underline and newer styled underline variants, so it is the safe cleanup sequence after curly, dotted, dashed, or colored underline output.
sgr.selective-reset.underline.probe: Send \x1b[4mX\x1b[24mY, verify the second cell has no underline.

sgr.selective-reset.inverse.name: Reset inverse (SGR 27)
sgr.selective-reset.inverse.body: SGR 27 resets the inverse video (SGR 7) attribute, restoring the original foreground/background color relationship. The sequence is ESC [ 27 m. Inverse video was one of the hardware-era highlight mechanisms, long before terminals had theme-aware selection colors. SGR 27 matters because status bars, menus, and cursor renderers often combine inverse video with explicit colors; using SGR 0 would clear every attribute, while SGR 27 restores only the foreground/background relationship.
sgr.selective-reset.inverse.probe: Send \x1b[7mX\x1b[27mY, verify the second cell has inverse=false.

sgr.selective-reset.italic.name: Reset italic (SGR 23)
sgr.selective-reset.italic.body: SGR 23 resets the italic (SGR 3) attribute, returning text to upright rendering. The sequence is ESC [ 23 m. This is a targeted reset that does not affect other active attributes like bold, underline, or colors. Italic reset became more visible once terminal fonts gained real italic variants. A targeted SGR 23 lets syntax highlighters and markdown renderers end emphasis without disturbing color, underline, or bold state. Older terminals that fake or ignore italic should still parse and clear the attribute cleanly.
sgr.selective-reset.italic.probe: Send \x1b[3mX\x1b[23mY, verify the second cell has italic=false.

sgr.reset.name: Reset all (SGR 0)
sgr.reset.body: SGR 0 resets all text attributes to their defaults: removes bold, faint, italic, underline, blink, inverse, hidden, strikethrough, overline, and resets foreground and background colors. The sequence is ESC [ 0 m or simply ESC [ m (parameter defaults to 0). Universally supported and the most fundamental SGR sequence.
sgr.reset.probe: Send \x1b[1;3;4mX\x1b[0mY, verify the second cell has bold, italic, and underline all cleared.

cursor.hide.name: Hide cursor (DECTCEM)
cursor.hide.body: DECTCEM (DEC Text Cursor Enable Mode) controls cursor visibility. Hide cursor: ESC [ ? 25 l. Show cursor: ESC [ ? 25 h. TUI applications typically hide the cursor during rendering to avoid flicker, then restore it at the final cursor position. Universally supported.
cursor.hide.probe: Send ESC [ ? 25 l (DECTCEM reset) and verify the cursor is no longer visible.

cursor.shape.name: Cursor shape (DECSCUSR)
cursor.shape.body: DECSCUSR (DEC Set Cursor Style) changes the cursor appearance. The sequence is ESC [ N SP q where N is: 0 or 1 (blinking block), 2 (steady block), 3 (blinking underline), 4 (steady underline), 5 (blinking bar/I-beam), 6 (steady bar). Applications use this to signal editing mode — for example, vim uses a block cursor in normal mode and a bar cursor in insert mode.
cursor.shape.probe: Send \x1b[6 q (DECSCUSR steady bar), verify cursor style is "beam".

cursor.move.absolute.name: Cursor position (CUP)
cursor.move.absolute.body: CUP (Cursor Position) moves the cursor to an absolute row and column. The sequence is ESC [ row ; col H, where row and column are 1-based. ESC [ H (no parameters) moves to the home position (1,1). This is the most fundamental cursor positioning command and is used by every full-screen TUI application.
cursor.move.absolute.probe: Send \x1b[5;10H (CUP), verify cursor is at row 4, col 9 (0-based). In app mode, query with DSR 6 and verify response \x1b[5;10R.

cursor.move.up.name: Cursor up (CUU)
cursor.move.up.body: CUU moves the cursor up by N rows without changing the column. The sequence is ESC [ N A (default N=1). The cursor stops at the top margin of the scroll region and does not wrap or scroll. CUU is relative movement within the active scrolling area. It is widely used by progress displays and TUIs that redraw a small region above the current cursor. A correct implementation clamps at the top margin instead of scrolling content, which distinguishes cursor movement from index/reverse-index operations.
cursor.move.up.probe: Move cursor down 5 then up 2 via \x1b[5B\x1b[2A, verify cursor row is 3 (0-based).

cursor.move.down.name: Cursor down (CUD)
cursor.move.down.body: CUD moves the cursor down by N rows without changing the column. The sequence is ESC [ N B (default N=1). The cursor stops at the bottom margin of the scroll region and does not wrap or scroll. CUD moves the cursor without producing text and without scrolling. That makes it different from LF or IND at the bottom margin. The distinction is important for terminal emulators: cursor motion should clamp inside the scrolling region, while line-feed-like operations may cause content to move.

cursor.move.forward.name: Cursor forward (CUF)
cursor.move.forward.body: CUF moves the cursor forward (right) by N columns. The sequence is ESC [ N C (default N=1). The cursor stops at the right edge of the screen and does not wrap to the next line. CUF is the relative counterpart to absolute cursor addressing. It is simple, but its edge behavior is important: moving past the right margin clamps instead of wrapping, which lets applications position safely near the edge of a pane. Wrapping is controlled by text output and DECAWM, not by CUF.

cursor.move.back.name: Cursor back (CUB)
cursor.move.back.body: CUB moves the cursor back (left) by N columns. The sequence is ESC [ N D (default N=1). The cursor stops at the left edge of the screen and does not wrap to the previous line. CUB is one of the small cursor-relative operations that let applications update in place without repainting a full screen. It is heavily used by line editors, prompts, spinners, and progress indicators. The no-wrap behavior is load-bearing: moving left from the first column should clamp, not jump into the previous row.
cursor.move.back.probe: Write "ABC" then send \x1b[2D (CUB 2), verify cursor column is 1 (0-based).

cursor.move.home.name: Cursor home
cursor.move.home.body: Moves the cursor to the home position (row 1, column 1). Equivalent to ESC [ H or ESC [ 1;1 H. This is a special case of CUP with no parameters. The parameterless form is a convenience alias for CUP row 1, column 1. Coordinates in ECMA-48 and DEC manuals are 1-based, which is a common off-by-one trap for emulator authors building zero-based internal grids. When origin mode is enabled, CUP-style addressing is relative to the scroll region rather than the full screen.
cursor.move.home.probe: Write "ABC" then send \x1b[H (CUP no params), verify cursor is at (0, 0).

cursor.horizontal-absolute.name: Cursor horizontal absolute (CHA)
cursor.horizontal-absolute.body: CHA moves the cursor to an absolute column on the current row. The sequence is ESC [ N G where N is the 1-based column number (default 1). Unlike CUP, this only changes the horizontal position, leaving the row unchanged. CHA is a compact alternative to CUP when only the column changes. It is handy for prompts, progress tables, and status rows that rewrite fields on the current line. Like other ECMA-48 coordinates, the parameter is 1-based, so column 1 means the leftmost cell.
cursor.horizontal-absolute.probe: Write "ABCDE" then send \x1b[3G (CHA), verify cursor column is 2 (0-based).

cursor.next-line.name: Cursor next line (CNL)
cursor.next-line.body: CNL moves the cursor to the beginning of the line N rows down. The sequence is ESC [ N E (default N=1). Unlike CUD, which preserves the column, CNL always sets the column to 1. CNL combines vertical movement with carriage-return behavior: it moves down and returns to column 1. That makes it convenient for generated output that wants to start fresh rows without emitting separate CUD and CR operations. It is less common than CUP or plain newline, so it is a useful parser-completeness check.
cursor.next-line.probe: Write "ABC" then send \x1b[2E (CNL 2), verify cursor is at row 2, column 0.

cursor.position-report.name: Cursor position report (DSR 6)
cursor.position-report.body: DSR 6 (Device Status Report) queries the current cursor position. The application sends ESC [ 6 n and the terminal responds with ESC [ row ; col R. This request-response mechanism is used by shell integration scripts, TUI frameworks, and applications that need to know where the cursor is before rendering content.
cursor.position-report.probe: Move cursor to row 3, col 5 via \x1b[3;5H, send DSR 6 (\x1b[6n), verify response contains 3;5R.
cursor.position-report.sequence: ESC [ 6 n → ESC [ row ; col R

cursor.save-restore.name: Save/restore cursor (DECSC)
cursor.save-restore.body: DECSC saves the cursor position and attributes; DECRC restores them. Save: ESC 7. Restore: ESC 8. Also available as CSI variants: ESC [ s (save) and ESC [ u (restore). The saved state includes cursor position, character attributes, character set, and origin mode. Applications use this to temporarily move the cursor for status updates without disrupting the main content area.
cursor.save-restore.probe: Write "AB", save with \x1b7 (DECSC), move to (5,5), restore with \x1b8 (DECRC), verify cursor returned to col 2, row 0.

cursor.ansi-save.name: ANSI save cursor (CSI s)
cursor.ansi-save.body: ANSI save cursor (CSI s, i.e. ESC [ s) saves the current cursor position. This is the SCO/ANSI variant of cursor save and is distinct from DECSC (ESC 7): the ANSI form only saves the cursor position (row, column), while DECSC additionally saves character attributes, the active character set, and origin mode. The sequence collides with DECSLRM (Set Left/Right Margins, also CSI Pl ; Pr s) — when DECLRMM (mode 69) is enabled, terminals interpret CSI s with parameters as DECSLRM and the parameter-less form as save cursor. Modern xterm-derived terminals all implement this disambiguation, but some legacy terminals may treat CSI s only as DECSLRM.
cursor.ansi-save.probe: Position cursor at (3,5), send \x1b[s (CSI s), move to (10,10), send \x1b[u (CSI u) to restore, verify cursor is back at (3,5).

cursor.ansi-restore.name: ANSI restore cursor (CSI u)
cursor.ansi-restore.body: ANSI restore cursor (CSI u, i.e. ESC [ u) restores the cursor position previously saved with ANSI save cursor (CSI s). This is the SCO/ANSI variant of cursor restore and is distinct from DECRC (ESC 8): the ANSI form only restores cursor position, while DECRC also restores character attributes, character set, and origin mode. Note that CSI u is also used by the Kitty keyboard protocol as a key event reporting sequence (with parameters and a different context). When sent as input from terminal to application it's a key report; when sent as output from application to terminal it's a cursor restore. Most xterm-derived terminals implement both meanings unambiguously based on direction.
cursor.ansi-restore.probe: Position cursor at (3,5), send \x1b[s to save, move to (10,10), send \x1b[u (CSI u) to restore, verify cursor returned to (3,5).

cursor.reverse-wrap.name: Reverse Wrap (Mode 45)
cursor.reverse-wrap.body:
Reverse wrap mode (mode 45) allows backspace at column 0 to wrap to the end of the previous line. Without this mode, backspace at column 0 is a no-op. Useful for command-line editing across wrapped lines.

Reverse wrap is an xterm compatibility mode for editing across visually wrapped lines. It lets a backspace at the left edge move to the previous row's right edge, matching how users expect command-line editing to behave on soft-wrapped input. Because it changes a normally clamped control character, applications should not assume it is enabled.
cursor.reverse-wrap.probe: Enable auto-wrap + reverse wrap, fill row 0 to trigger wrap to row 1, backspace — verify cursor reverse-wraps to end of row 0 via getCursor().

cursor.cup-boundaries.name: CUP at screen boundaries
cursor.cup-boundaries.body: CUP (Cursor Position, CSI row ; col H) with out-of-bounds parameters should clamp the cursor to the screen edges rather than wrapping or producing undefined behavior. Sending CSI 999 ; 999 H should place the cursor at the last row and last column of the visible screen. This is important for applications that use CUP with large values as a shorthand for "move to bottom-right corner" without querying screen dimensions first.
cursor.cup-boundaries.probe: Send \x1b[999;999H, verify cursor is at (rows-1, cols-1) — not beyond the screen boundaries.

cursor.cuu-past-top.name: CUU stops at top
cursor.cuu-past-top.body: CUU (Cursor Up, CSI Pn A) should stop at the top margin of the scroll region, or row 1 if no scroll region is set. Moving up with a count exceeding the current row should clamp to row 0 rather than wrapping to the bottom. This prevents cursor escape from the visible area and is relied upon by screen-clearing routines that use large CUU counts.
cursor.cuu-past-top.probe: Position at row 3, send \x1b[999A (CUU with count 999), verify cursor stopped at row 0.

cursor.cud-past-bottom.name: CUD stops at bottom
cursor.cud-past-bottom.body: CUD (Cursor Down, CSI Pn B) should stop at the bottom margin of the scroll region, or the last row if no scroll region is set. Moving down with a count exceeding the remaining rows should clamp to the last row rather than scrolling or wrapping. Unlike a linefeed at the bottom margin, CUD never triggers scrolling — it simply stops.
cursor.cud-past-bottom.probe: Position at row 0, send \x1b[999B (CUD with count 999), verify cursor stopped at the last row.

cursor.cup-scroll-region.name: CUP with DECOM
cursor.cup-scroll-region.body: When DECOM (Origin Mode, CSI ? 6 h) is set, CUP coordinates become relative to the current scroll region set by DECSTBM. With a scroll region of rows 5-15 and DECOM enabled, CSI 1 ; 1 H moves the cursor to row 5 (the top of the scroll region) rather than the absolute top of the screen. This interaction between DECSTBM and DECOM is fundamental to how full-screen applications partition the terminal into independently scrolling regions.
cursor.cup-scroll-region.probe: Set DECSTBM 5;15, enable DECOM, send \x1b[1;1H, verify cursor is at row 4 (0-based scroll region top). Disable DECOM and reset scroll region.

cursor.vpa.name: VPA vertical position absolute (CSI d)
cursor.vpa.body: VPA (Vertical Position Absolute) moves the cursor to an absolute row without changing the column. The sequence is CSI Ps d where Ps is the 1-based row number (default 1). VPA is the vertical counterpart of CHA (Cursor Horizontal Absolute, CSI G) — together they allow independent positioning on each axis without using the full CUP (CSI H) sequence.
cursor.vpa.probe: Position cursor at row 3, col 5, then send \x1b[10d (VPA row 10), verify cursor moved to row 9 (0-based) while column remained at 5.

cursor.cpl.name: CPL cursor preceding line (CSI F)
cursor.cpl.body: CPL (Cursor Preceding Line) moves the cursor to the beginning of the line N rows up. The sequence is CSI Ps F (default Ps=1). Unlike CUU, which preserves the column, CPL always sets the column to 1. CPL is the complement of CNL (Cursor Next Line, CSI E) — CNL moves down, CPL moves up, and both reset the column to the left margin.
cursor.cpl.probe: Position cursor at row 5, col 10, send \x1b[2F (CPL 2), verify cursor is at row 3, column 0.

cursor.hpa.name: HPA horizontal position absolute (CSI `)
cursor.hpa.body: HPA (Horizontal Position Absolute) moves the cursor to an absolute column on the current row. The sequence is CSI Ps ` where Ps is the 1-based column number (default 1). HPA is functionally equivalent to CHA (CSI G) but uses a different final character. Both are defined in ECMA-48 — some older terminals only implement one of the two.
cursor.hpa.probe: Write "ABCDEFGH" then send \x1b[5` (HPA col 5), verify cursor column is 4 (0-based).

erase.line.right.name: Erase to EOL (EL 0)
erase.line.right.body: EL 0 erases from the cursor position to the end of the line. The sequence is ESC [ 0 K or ESC [ K (parameter defaults to 0). This is the most commonly used erase sequence — TUI applications use it after writing content on a line to clear any leftover characters from a previous render.
erase.line.right.probe: Write "XXXXX", move to col 1 via \x1b[1G, send \x1b[K (EL 0), verify cell at (0,0) is blank.

erase.line.left.name: Erase to BOL (EL 1)
erase.line.left.body: EL 1 erases from the beginning of the line to the cursor position (inclusive). The sequence is ESC [ 1 K. Less commonly used than EL 0 (erase to end of line). EL 1 is the mirror image of the common EL 0 erase-to-end operation. It is useful for redrawing the left side of a prompt or clearing a partially typed command while preserving content to the right. Because the cursor does not move, applications can combine it with precise cursor addressing instead of repainting an entire row.
erase.line.left.probe: Write "XXXXX", move to col 3, send \x1b[1K (EL 1), verify cell at (0,0) is blank.

erase.line.all.name: Erase line (EL 2)
erase.line.all.body: EL 2 erases the entire current line. The sequence is ESC [ 2 K. The cursor position is not changed. Used to clear a full line before rewriting it. EL belongs to the ECMA-48 erase family inherited by VT-style terminals. Full-line erase is commonly paired with carriage return when redrawing progress bars, prompts, and status rows. A useful compatibility check is that EL clears cells without moving the cursor, because applications often erase and then immediately write replacement content at the same location.
erase.line.all.probe: Write "XXXXX", send \x1b[2K (EL 2), verify cell at (0,0) is blank.

erase.screen.above.name: Erase above (ED 1)
erase.screen.above.body: ED 1 erases from the beginning of the screen to the cursor position (inclusive). The sequence is ESC [ 1 J. Clears all content above and to the left of the cursor. ED 1 is less visible than clear-screen operations, but it completes the ECMA-48 erase triad: below, above, and all. Full-screen applications use it when restoring or redrawing only part of a viewport. Like the rest of the ED family, it acts on displayed cells and should not be confused with scrollback erasure.
erase.screen.above.probe: Write three lines, position cursor at row 3 col 2, send \x1b[1J (ED 1), verify cell at (0,0) is blank.

erase.screen.all.name: Erase screen (ED 2)
erase.screen.all.body: ED 2 erases the entire visible screen. The sequence is ESC [ 2 J. The cursor position is not changed. Does not affect the scrollback buffer — use ED 3 to clear scrollback. ED 2 is what many users think of as 'clear screen', but it only clears the visible display. It intentionally does not erase scrollback; xterm-style ED 3 exists for that separate job. This distinction matters for security and debugging, because wiping the viewport should not silently destroy the user's history.
erase.screen.all.probe: Write three lines, send \x1b[2J (ED 2), verify all visible content is cleared.

erase.screen.below.name: Erase below (ED 0)
erase.screen.below.body: ED 0 erases from the cursor position to the end of the screen. The sequence is ESC [ 0 J or ESC [ J (parameter defaults to 0). Commonly used to clear the screen below a status bar or after repositioning content. ED 0 is the default ED operation and one of the most common screen-cleanup sequences. Shell prompts, pagers, and TUIs use it after moving the cursor to a known point so stale content below the new layout disappears. Like other erase operations, it should leave cursor position unchanged.
erase.screen.below.probe: Write three lines, move home, send \x1b[J (ED 0), verify line 2 content is gone.

erase.screen.scrollback.name: Erase scrollback (ED 3)
erase.screen.scrollback.body: ED 3 clears the scrollback buffer. The sequence is ESC [ 3 J. An xterm extension not part of ECMA-48. Applications may use this for security purposes (clearing sensitive output from scrollback after a password prompt) or to implement a "clear" command that truly clears all terminal history.
erase.screen.scrollback.probe: Write 30 lines to fill scrollback, send \x1b[3J (ED 3), verify total line count is at most screen height.

erase.character.name: Erase character (ECH)
erase.character.body: ECH replaces N characters at the cursor position with blank spaces. The sequence is ESC [ N X (default N=1). Unlike delete (DCH), erase does not shift subsequent characters — it overwrites in place. The cursor position is not changed. ECH is an in-place eraser: it blanks cells without shifting the rest of the row. That makes it different from DCH, which deletes and pulls later cells left. The distinction matters for editors and form UIs that clear a fixed-width field while leaving surrounding content exactly where it is.
erase.character.probe: Write "ABCDE", move to col 1, send \x1b[3X (ECH 3), verify first 3 cells are blank and "D" remains at col 3.

erase.selective.name: Selective Erase (DECSED)
erase.selective.body:
DECSED (CSI ? Ps J) erases only unprotected characters, leaving characters with the protected attribute (DECSCA, SGR 1 m) intact. Used for form-based applications where static labels should survive screen clears.

Selective erase belongs to DEC's form-oriented terminal model. Protected fields let host applications clear user-entered data while preserving labels and layout, a pattern common in data-entry systems before graphical forms. It survives mostly in xterm and high-compatibility emulators, but it explains why protected attributes exist at all.
erase.selective.probe: Write "ABCDE", move cursor home, send DECSED (CSI ? 2 J), verify the text is erased. Confirms selective erase display is recognized.

erase.el-with-attrs.name: EL erases with bg color
erase.el-with-attrs.body: EL (Erase in Line) should fill erased cells with the current background color, not the default background. When a background color is set via SGR (e.g., SGR 42 for green) before issuing EL, the erased area should be painted with that background color. This is how TUI applications create colored status bars, highlighted regions, and filled backgrounds — they set a background color and erase the line to paint the entire line with that color.
erase.el-with-attrs.probe: Set green background with \x1b[42m, write text, move to start of line, send \x1b[K (EL 0), verify erased cells have the green background color.

erase.ed-scroll-region.name: ED at scroll region boundary
erase.ed-scroll-region.body: ED (Erase in Display) behavior when a scroll region (DECSTBM) is active varies by terminal. Some terminals erase only within the scroll region, while others erase the full screen regardless. The key edge case is that content outside the scroll region should generally be preserved by ED operations performed inside the region. This matters for applications that use scroll regions with fixed header/footer areas.
erase.ed-scroll-region.probe: Write "KEEP_THIS" on row 0, set scroll region 3;10, move cursor inside region, send \x1b[J (ED 0), verify row 0 still contains "KEEP_THIS".

editing.insert-chars.name: Insert characters (ICH)
editing.insert-chars.body: ICH inserts N blank characters at the cursor position, shifting existing characters to the right. The sequence is ESC [ N @ (default N=1). Characters pushed past the right margin are lost. Used by editors (vim, emacs) and terminal multiplexers (tmux, screen) for inline text insertion without redrawing the entire line.
editing.insert-chars.probe: Write "ABCDE", move to col 1, send \x1b[2@ (ICH 2), verify 2 blanks inserted and "A" shifted to col 2.

editing.delete-chars.name: Delete characters (DCH)
editing.delete-chars.body: DCH deletes N characters at the cursor position, shifting subsequent characters to the left. The sequence is ESC [ N P (default N=1). Blank characters are inserted at the right margin to fill the vacated space. The complement of ICH (insert characters).
editing.delete-chars.probe: Write "ABCDE", move to col 1, send \x1b[2P (DCH 2), verify "C" is now at col 0.

editing.insert-lines.name: Insert lines (IL)
editing.insert-lines.body: IL inserts N blank lines at the cursor row, scrolling existing lines down within the scroll region. The sequence is ESC [ N L (default N=1). Lines scrolled past the bottom margin are lost. Used by full-screen applications to make room for new content without redrawing the entire screen.
editing.insert-lines.probe: Write 3 lines, move to row 2, send \x1b[1L (IL 1), verify row 1 is blank and original content shifted down.

editing.delete-lines.name: Delete lines (DL)
editing.delete-lines.body: DL deletes N lines at the cursor row, scrolling lines below upward within the scroll region. The sequence is ESC [ N M (default N=1). Blank lines are inserted at the bottom margin. The complement of IL (insert lines). DL is part of the terminal editing toolbox that predates full-screen repainting. Instead of redrawing every row, an application can delete lines inside the scroll region and let the terminal shift the remaining content. It is still useful for efficient editors, but correctness depends on honoring margins and inserting blank rows at the far edge.
editing.delete-lines.probe: Write 3 lines, move to row 2, send \x1b[1M (DL 1), verify LINE3 moved up to row 1.

editing.repeat-char.name: Repeat character (REP)
editing.repeat-char.body: REP repeats the preceding graphic character N times. The sequence is ESC [ N b (default N=1). This is a bandwidth optimization — instead of sending the same character hundreds of times (e.g., filling a row with dashes), the application sends the character once followed by REP. Not all terminals implement this sequence.
editing.repeat-char.probe: Write "X" then send \x1b[4b (REP 4), verify 5 consecutive X characters (1 original + 4 repeats).

editing.sl.name: SL — shift left (CSI Ps SP @)
editing.sl.body: SL (Shift Left) horizontally scrolls the display contents left by N columns within the current scroll region. The sequence is ESC [ Ps SP @ — note the literal space character before @, which distinguishes SL from ICH (ESC [ Ps @). Default Ps=1. Columns shifted off the left edge are discarded; blank columns are inserted at the right edge. SL is the horizontal counterpart of SU (Scroll Up) and is rarely used in modern TUI applications — most full-screen apps redraw their content rather than rely on terminal-side horizontal scrolling. Some xterm-derived terminals implement it for compatibility with applications that drive horizontal panning displays. Originally specified in ECMA-48 alongside SU/SD but not implemented by all terminals.
editing.sl.probe: Write "ABCDEFGH" on row 1, send \x1b[2 @ (SL 2), verify the sequence is consumed without leaving literal characters on screen. Most headless backends don't implement column shifts, so verification is partial.

editing.sr.name: SR — shift right (CSI Ps SP A)
editing.sr.body: SR (Shift Right) horizontally scrolls the display contents right by N columns within the current scroll region. The sequence is ESC [ Ps SP A — note the literal space character before A, which distinguishes SR from CUU (ESC [ Ps A). Default Ps=1. Columns shifted off the right edge are discarded; blank columns are inserted at the left edge. SR is the horizontal counterpart of SD (Scroll Down) and the inverse of SL (Shift Left). Like SL, it's rarely used in modern terminal applications and many emulators either don't implement it or treat it as a no-op. Specified in ECMA-48 alongside SU/SD/SL.
editing.sr.probe: Write "ABCDEFGH" on row 1, send \x1b[2 A (SR 2), verify the sequence is consumed without leaving literal characters on screen. Most headless backends don't implement column shifts, so verification is partial.

editing.decic.name: DECIC — insert columns (CSI Ps ' })
editing.decic.body: DECIC (DEC Insert Column) inserts N blank columns at the cursor's column position, shifting all content to the right of the cursor further right. The sequence is ESC [ Ps ' } — note the apostrophe (intermediate) before }. Default Ps=1. Columns shifted past the right margin are discarded. DECIC is the horizontal/column counterpart of IL (Insert Lines): IL inserts blank rows by shifting content down, DECIC inserts blank columns by shifting content right. It only operates on cells within the current scroll region (top/bottom margins) and the current left/right margin region if DECLRMM (mode 69) is enabled. Originally a VT420 feature, supported by xterm and some other modern terminals but not universally implemented.
editing.decic.probe: Write content on multiple rows, position cursor in the middle, send \x1b[2'} (DECIC 2), verify the sequence is consumed without leaving literal characters on screen. Most headless backends don't implement column inserts, so verification is partial.

editing.decdc.name: DECDC — delete columns (CSI Ps ' ~)
editing.decdc.body: DECDC (DEC Delete Column) deletes N columns at the cursor's column position, shifting all content to the right of the cursor further left. The sequence is ESC [ Ps ' ~ — note the apostrophe (intermediate) before ~. Default Ps=1. Blank columns are inserted at the right margin to fill the vacated space. DECDC is the horizontal counterpart of DL (Delete Lines) and the inverse of DECIC (Insert Column). It only operates within the current scroll region's vertical bounds, and within the left/right margin bounds when DECLRMM (mode 69) is enabled. Originally a VT420 feature, supported by xterm but rarely used in practice — most TUI applications redraw their content rather than use column-based editing.
editing.decdc.probe: Write content on multiple rows, position cursor in the middle, send \x1b[2'~ (DECDC 2), verify the sequence is consumed without leaving literal characters on screen. Most headless backends don't implement column deletes, so verification is partial.

editing.decfra.name: DECFRA — fill rectangular area
editing.decfra.body: DECFRA (CSI Pc ; Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr $ x) fills a rectangular area with character Pc from rows Pt through Pb and columns Pl through Pr. Introduced on the DEC VT420 in 1990 for form-fill applications and spreadsheets that needed to paint rectangular regions of the screen — drawing dividers, padding fields, or clearing a region with a fill character — without touching cells outside the rectangle. xterm and mintty implement DECFRA; nearly every other modern terminal skips it. The character must be in the printable ASCII range or DEC supplemental graphic range.
editing.decfra.probe: Send \x1b[88;1;1;3;5$x (fill rows 1-3, cols 1-5 with 'X'), verify cells in the rectangle contain 'X'.
editing.decfra.sequence: CSI Pc ; Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr $ x

editing.decera.name: DECERA — erase rectangular area
editing.decera.body: DECERA (CSI Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr $ z) erases a rectangular region from rows Pt-Pb, columns Pl-Pr, replacing each cell with a space and the current SGR background. Unlike a global ED, DECERA leaves protected characters (set via DECSCA) untouched, making it safe inside form-fill applications that have static labels alongside editable fields. Companion to DECFRA. Another VT420-era feature surviving mostly in xterm and mintty.
editing.decera.probe: Write text across multiple rows, send \x1b[1;1;3;5$z (erase rows 1-3, cols 1-5), verify cells in the rectangle are blank.
editing.decera.sequence: CSI Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr $ z

editing.decsera.name: DECSERA — selective erase rectangular area
editing.decsera.body: DECSERA (CSI Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr $ {) is the selective-erase variant of DECERA: it erases unprotected characters within the rectangle but leaves any cell marked with the DECSCA character protection attribute intact. This is the rectangular companion to DECSED (selective erase in display). On VT420-era hardware, form designers used DECSCA to protect field labels and DECSERA to clear input fields between records without disturbing the form structure.
editing.decsera.probe: Write text across multiple rows, send \x1b[1;1;3;5${ (selective erase rows 1-3, cols 1-5), verify the sequence is consumed without the literal characters appearing.

editing.deccra.name: DECCRA — copy rectangular area
editing.deccra.body: DECCRA (CSI Pts ; Pls ; Pbs ; Prs ; Pps ; Ptd ; Pld $ v) copies a rectangular area from one location to another. The source rectangle is Pts;Pls-Pbs;Prs on page Pps; the destination's top-left is Ptd;Pld. The most complex of the VT420 rectangle operations: cells are copied including their SGR attributes, source and destination may overlap (the result is as if the source were captured first), and the operation can cross page boundaries on terminals that implement multi-page memory. xterm implements all of this. Almost nothing else does.
editing.deccra.probe: Write text in a region, send \x1b[1;1;2;5;1;5;10$v (copy rows 1-2 cols 1-5 to row 5 col 10), verify the sequence is consumed.
editing.deccra.sequence: CSI Pts ; Pls ; Pbs ; Prs ; Pps ; Ptd ; Pld $ v

editing.deccara.name: DECCARA — change attrs in rectangle
editing.deccara.body: DECCARA (CSI Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr ; Ps $ r) changes the SGR attributes of every cell in a rectangular region without touching the characters themselves. The Ps parameters are SGR codes (1=bold, 4=underline, 7=inverse, etc.), accumulated and applied to the rectangle. Whether DECCARA operates on a true rectangle or on the stream of cells between the corners depends on DECSACE. Used by VT420-era forms to highlight an entire input field, error message, or table cell without rewriting its contents.
editing.deccara.probe: Write text, send \x1b[1;1;3;5;7$r (apply inverse to rows 1-3 cols 1-5), verify the sequence is consumed.
editing.deccara.sequence: CSI Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr ; Ps $ r

editing.decrara.name: DECRARA — reverse attrs in rectangle
editing.decrara.body: DECRARA (CSI Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr ; Ps $ t) toggles the listed SGR attributes within a rectangular region. Each cell flips the listed bits — bold cells become non-bold, non-bold cells become bold, and so on. Like DECCARA, it leaves the characters alone and obeys DECSACE to operate on a rectangle or a stream. VT420-era applications used DECRARA to flash highlight a field on validation errors or to invert a selected row in a list.
editing.decrara.probe: Write text, send \x1b[1;1;3;5;7$t (toggle inverse on rows 1-3 cols 1-5), verify the sequence is consumed.
editing.decrara.sequence: CSI Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr ; Ps $ t

editing.decsace.name: DECSACE — select attribute-change extent
editing.decsace.body: DECSACE (CSI Ps * x) selects how DECCARA and DECRARA interpret their coordinates. Ps=1 (the default) treats the corners as endpoints of a stream of cells in scan order, so the operation affects the partial rows at the top and bottom plus every cell on the rows between. Ps=2 treats the coordinates as a true rectangle, affecting only the cells inside the box. Modern users almost always want the rectangle interpretation; the stream default is a relic of how DEC originally specified the operations.
editing.decsace.probe: Send \x1b[2*x (select rectangle extent), verify the sequence is consumed.

editing.decrqcra.name: DECRQCRA — checksum rectangular area
editing.decrqcra.body: DECRQCRA (CSI Pid ; Ppid ; Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr * y) computes a 16-bit checksum of the cells in a rectangular region and reports it back to the host as a DCS response (DCS Pid ! ~ checksum ST). Pid is a request identifier echoed in the response; Ppid is the page number. The checksum hashes both characters and SGR attributes, which is why it's used by DEC's terminal conformance test suites — a test script can paint a known pattern, ask the terminal for the checksum, and compare against an expected value to confirm the terminal rendered the pattern correctly. xterm still implements this. Almost no application has ever used it outside of test suites.
editing.decrqcra.probe: Send \x1b[1;1;1;1;1;1*y (checksum cell 1,1), verify the sequence is consumed without literal output.
editing.decrqcra.sequence: CSI Pid ; Ppid ; Pt ; Pl ; Pb ; Pr * y

input.modify-other-keys.name: modifyOtherKeys
input.modify-other-keys.body:
modifyOtherKeys (CSI > 4 ; Pv m) enables extended key reporting that distinguishes modified key combinations that would otherwise be ambiguous. At level 2, keys like Ctrl+I are reported distinctly from Tab, and Ctrl+M from Enter.

modifyOtherKeys is xterm's opt-in path for less ambiguous keyboard reporting. It predates the Kitty keyboard protocol and is still relevant because many applications learned to parse it first. The tradeoff is compatibility: once enabled, key sequences change shape, so applications must enable it deliberately and restore terminal state on exit.
input.modify-other-keys.probe: Check if the terminal supports xterm modifyOtherKeys mode for enhanced key reporting.

input.csi-u.name: CSI u Key Encoding
input.csi-u.body:
The fixterms/CSI u encoding reports keypresses as CSI codepoint ; modifiers u sequences. This was the precursor to the Kitty keyboard protocol and provides unambiguous key identification for terminal applications.

CSI u came from the 'fixterms' effort to repair ambiguous keyboard input without replacing the whole terminal protocol. It reports Unicode code points plus modifiers, making Ctrl-I distinguishable from Tab and Ctrl-M from Enter. Kitty's keyboard protocol is the richer successor, but CSI u remains an important compatibility step.
input.csi-u.probe: Query keyboard mode flags and check if the terminal supports CSI u encoding for key events.

input.pixel-mouse.name: Pixel Mouse Reporting (1016)
input.pixel-mouse.body:
SGR-Pixels mouse mode (mode 1016) reports mouse coordinates in pixels rather than cells. This enables sub-cell precision for applications that need exact pointer positioning, such as terminal-based drawing tools or image viewers.

Pixel mouse mode is the precision extension to SGR mouse reporting. Cell coordinates are enough for text widgets, but drawing tools, image viewers, and high-DPI terminal UIs need sub-cell location. The mode uses the familiar SGR mouse response shape, replacing cell units with pixel units.
input.pixel-mouse.probe: Enable SGR pixel mouse (CSI ? 1016 h), check getMode("pixelMouse") returns true.

input.urxvt-mouse.name: urxvt Mouse Reporting (1015)
input.urxvt-mouse.body:
urxvt-style mouse reporting (mode 1015) uses decimal encoding for coordinates, extending reporting beyond the 223-column limit of traditional X10 encoding. Less common than SGR mouse (1006) but still supported by some terminals.

URxvt mode was an intermediate answer to xterm's coordinate limit: it kept the mouse idea but reported decimal coordinates. SGR mouse later became the cleaner and more widely adopted version, so mode 1015 is mostly legacy. Testing it still catches parsers that hard-code only the oldest byte-encoded mouse format.
input.urxvt-mouse.probe: Enable urxvt mouse mode (CSI ? 1015 h), check getMode("mouseTracking") returns true. Verifies the backend recognizes this specific mouse encoding.

input.x10-mouse.name: X10 Mouse Tracking (9)
input.x10-mouse.body:
X10 compatibility mouse tracking (mode 9) reports only button press events, not releases or motion. This is the oldest mouse tracking mode, limited to coordinates up to 223 and press-only events.

X10 mode is the ancestor of terminal mouse reporting. It came from xterm and reports only button presses using compact byte-encoded coordinates, which creates the classic 223-column limit. Modern applications should prefer SGR mouse, but X10 remains useful for understanding the compatibility stack that later mouse modes extended.
input.x10-mouse.probe: Enable X10 mouse mode (CSI ? 9 h), check getMode("mouseTracking") returns true.

input.button-event-mouse.name: Button-Event Mouse (1002)
input.button-event-mouse.body:
Button-event tracking (mode 1002) reports mouse button press, release, and motion while a button is held. More detailed than basic tracking (1000) but less verbose than all-motion tracking (1003).

Mode 1002 was one of xterm's steps from click-only reporting toward interactive mouse tracking. It reports drags only while a button is held, which is enough for selection, resize handles, and sliders without the volume of all-motion mode. The later SGR coordinate encoding made the same event model practical on large terminals.
input.button-event-mouse.probe: Enable button-event mouse tracking (CSI ? 1002 h), check getMode("mouseTracking") returns true.

modes.alt-screen.enter.name: Enter alt screen (DECSET 1049)
modes.alt-screen.enter.body: DECSET 1049 switches to the alternate screen buffer and saves the cursor position. The sequence is ESC [ ? 1049 h. The alternate screen is a separate buffer used by full-screen TUI applications (vim, less, htop, tmux) so they can render without disturbing the user's scrollback history. When the application exits and sends DECRST 1049, the terminal restores the original buffer contents and cursor position — the user's previous shell output reappears as if the TUI never ran. This is why closing vim brings back the terminal content that was there before. Mode 1049 combines three operations: save cursor (DECSC), switch to alt screen (?47), and clear the alt screen. Universally supported in modern terminals.

modes.alt-screen.exit.name: Exit alt screen (DECRST 1049)
modes.alt-screen.exit.body: DECRST 1049 exits the alternate screen buffer and restores the cursor position. The sequence is ESC [ ? 1049 l. Restores the main screen buffer, including all scrollback content that was present before the application entered the alternate screen.
modes.alt-screen.exit.probe: Enter then exit alt screen via \x1b[?1049h / \x1b[?1049l, verify altScreen mode is off.

modes.bracketed-paste.name: Bracketed paste (DECSET 2004)
modes.bracketed-paste.body: Bracketed paste mode wraps pasted text in special escape sequences so applications can distinguish pasted content from typed input. Enable with ESC [ ? 2004 h. When active, pasted text is bracketed between ESC [ 200 ~ (start) and ESC [ 201 ~ (end). This is a security feature: without bracketed paste, pasting text containing newlines into a shell prompt would immediately execute commands. With bracketed paste, the shell can treat the entire paste as literal text. This also enables editors to handle paste correctly — vim can enter paste mode automatically, preserving indentation rather than auto-indenting each line. Widely adopted. Most modern shells (bash, zsh, fish) and editors (vim, emacs) enable bracketed paste by default.

modes.synchronized-output.name: Synchronized output (DECSET 2026)
modes.synchronized-output.body: Synchronized output prevents flicker during screen updates by batching terminal output. The application sends ESC [ ? 2026 h before a render and ESC [ ? 2026 l after. The terminal buffers all output between these markers and renders it as a single atomic frame. Without synchronized output, complex TUI updates (clearing screen, repositioning cursor, writing content) may partially render between frames, causing visible tearing or flicker. This is especially noticeable over slow connections or with complex layouts. This protocol was proposed by the terminal-wg (Terminal Working Group) and adopted by Ghostty, Kitty, WezTerm, foot, Contour, and other modern terminals. It has become the standard way to achieve flicker-free TUI rendering.
modes.synchronized-output.probe: Send \x1b[?2026h, write text, send \x1b[?2026l, verify output rendered without crash.

modes.mouse-tracking.name: Mouse tracking (DECSET 1000)
modes.mouse-tracking.body: DECSET 1000 enables basic mouse click reporting (Normal tracking mode). The sequence is ESC [ ? 1000 h. When enabled, button press and release events are reported to the application. The legacy X10 encoding reports coordinates as single bytes offset by 32, limiting reports to the first 223 columns/rows. For larger terminals, use SGR mouse mode (DECSET 1006) instead.

modes.mouse-sgr.name: SGR mouse mode (DECSET 1006)
modes.mouse-sgr.body: SGR mouse mode replaces the legacy X10 mouse encoding with a format that supports arbitrarily large coordinates. Enable with ESC [ ? 1006 h (in addition to a tracking mode like ?1000 or ?1003). Mouse events are reported as ESC [ < button ; col ; row M for press and ESC [ < button ; col ; row m for release. The SGR format solves critical limitations of legacy mouse reporting: coordinates are decimal (no 223-column limit), button press and release are distinguishable by the final character (M vs m), and the format is unambiguous (no byte-value collisions with control characters). All modern TUI frameworks use SGR mouse mode.
modes.mouse-sgr.probe: Enable SGR mouse (CSI ? 1006 h), check getMode("sgrMouse") returns true. Confirms the backend tracks SGR mouse encoding mode.

modes.mouse-all.name: All motion mouse (DECSET 1003)
modes.mouse-all.body: DECSET 1003 enables all-motion mouse tracking — the terminal reports mouse movement events even when no button is pressed. The sequence is ESC [ ? 1003 h. This generates significantly more traffic than normal tracking (?1000) or button-event tracking (?1002), so applications should only enable it when hover effects are needed.
modes.mouse-all.probe: Enable all-motion mouse (CSI ? 1003 h), check getMode("mouseTracking") returns true. Confirms the backend recognizes mode 1003.

modes.focus-tracking.name: Focus tracking (DECSET 1004)
modes.focus-tracking.body: DECSET 1004 enables focus in/out event reporting. The sequence is ESC [ ? 1004 h. When the terminal window gains focus, it sends ESC [ I; when it loses focus, it sends ESC [ O. Applications use this to pause animations, dim the UI, or refresh data when the user returns to the window.

modes.reverse-video.name: Reverse video (DECSCNM)
modes.reverse-video.body: DECSCNM (Screen Mode) swaps the default foreground and background colors for the entire screen. Enable with ESC [ ? 5 h, disable with ESC [ ? 5 l. This is a global mode that affects all displayed content, distinct from SGR 7 which only affects individual character cells.

modes.origin.name: Origin mode (DECOM)
modes.origin.body: DECOM (Origin Mode) changes how cursor positioning works relative to scroll margins. Enable with ESC [ ? 6 h. When active, cursor coordinates are relative to the scroll region rather than the entire screen, and the cursor is confined within the scroll region boundaries. Used by applications that operate within scroll regions to simplify coordinate calculations.
modes.origin.probe: Send \x1b[?6h (DECOM), verify originMode is active, then reset with \x1b[?6l.

modes.left-right-margin.name: Left/Right Margins (DECLRMM, Mode 69)
modes.left-right-margin.body:
DECLRMM (mode 69) enables left and right scroll margins, similar to top/bottom margins set by DECSTBM. When enabled, DECSLRM sets the left and right margins. Scrolling and editing operations are confined to the margin area.

Left/right margins extend DEC's top/bottom scroll-region idea into two dimensions. They are useful for forms, panes, and rectangular editing operations, but many modern terminals skip them because most contemporary TUIs manage rectangles in application code. Correct behavior requires editing and scrolling operations to honor both horizontal and vertical margins.
modes.left-right-margin.probe: Enable DECLRMM (CSI ? 69 h), check getMode("leftRightMargin") returns true. Confirms the backend tracks left/right margin mode.

modes.insert-replace.name: Insert/replace mode (IRM)
modes.insert-replace.body: IRM (Insert/Replace Mode) controls whether new characters insert (shifting existing content right) or replace (overwriting) at the cursor. Enable insert mode with ESC [ 4 h, disable (replace mode) with ESC [ 4 l. Replace mode is the default. Most modern TUI applications use explicit ICH/DCH sequences rather than IRM.
modes.insert-replace.probe: Write "ABC", move to col 1, send \x1b[4h (IRM), write "X", verify X is at col 0 and A shifted to col 1.

modes.application-cursor.name: App cursor keys (DECCKM)
modes.application-cursor.body: DECCKM changes the escape sequences generated by arrow keys. Enable with ESC [ ? 1 h. In normal mode, arrow keys send CSI sequences (ESC [ A); in application mode, they send SS3 sequences (ESC O A). Full-screen applications typically enable application cursor mode to get unambiguous key input.

modes.application-keypad.name: Application keypad (DECKPAM)
modes.application-keypad.body: DECKPAM switches the numeric keypad to application mode, where numpad keys send escape sequences instead of numeric characters. Enable with ESC =, disable with ESC >. This allows applications to distinguish numpad keys from the main keyboard number row.
modes.application-keypad.probe: Send \x1b= (DECKPAM), verify applicationKeypad is active. Then send \x1b> (DECKPNM), verify it is off.

modes.auto-wrap.name: Auto-wrap (DECAWM)
modes.auto-wrap.body: DECAWM controls whether text automatically wraps to the next line when it reaches the right margin. Enable with ESC [ ? 7 h (default on), disable with ESC [ ? 7 l. When disabled, characters written past the right margin overwrite the last column. Most terminals default to auto-wrap enabled.
modes.auto-wrap.probe: Write 80+ characters, verify text wraps to the next row.

modes.altscreen-47.name: Alt screen legacy (?47)
modes.altscreen-47.body: DECSET 47 is the original alternate screen buffer mode. The sequence is ESC [ ? 47 h to enter and ESC [ ? 47 l to exit. Unlike the modern ?1049 mode, ?47 does not save or restore the cursor position, and switching back does not clear the alt screen on the next entry. This legacy mode predates the unified ?1049 sequence (which combines DECSC + ?47 + clear). Modern applications should use ?1049 instead — ?47 is preserved for backwards compatibility with older xterm clients and historical TUI software. Most terminal emulators still implement it, though some treat it as an alias for ?1047.
modes.altscreen-47.probe: Send \x1b[?47h (DECSET 47), verify altScreen mode is active, then exit with \x1b[?47l.

modes.altscreen-1047.name: Alt screen clear-on-enter (?1047)
modes.altscreen-1047.body: DECSET 1047 enters the alternate screen buffer and clears it on entry. The sequence is ESC [ ? 1047 h to enter, ESC [ ? 1047 l to exit (which also clears the alt screen before switching back). Unlike ?1049, mode 1047 does not save or restore the cursor position — applications using ?1047 must combine it with explicit DECSC/DECRC (ESC 7 / ESC 8) if they want cursor preservation. Mode 1047 is one of the building blocks that mode 1049 composes (along with cursor save and clear). Most terminal emulators implement all three (?47, ?1047, ?1049) so legacy software keeps working alongside modern apps.

modes.altscreen-1048.name: Save/restore cursor (?1048)
modes.altscreen-1048.body: DECSET 1048 saves the current cursor position and attributes; DECRST 1048 restores them. The sequences are ESC [ ? 1048 h (save) and ESC [ ? 1048 l (restore). Unlike ?1049, this mode does not switch to the alternate screen — it only manipulates the cursor save/restore state. Mode 1048 is one of the three ingredients that ?1049 combines (cursor save + ?1047 alt screen + clear). Applications that need to checkpoint the cursor without entering the alt screen can use ?1048 directly, or use the older DECSC/DECRC (ESC 7 / ESC 8) which performs the same operation.
modes.altscreen-1048.probe: Position cursor, send \x1b[?1048h (save), move elsewhere, send \x1b[?1048l (restore), verify cursor returned to saved position.

modes.alt-scroll-1007.name: Alt-scroll mouse wheel (?1007)
modes.alt-scroll-1007.body: DECSET 1007 enables alternate scroll mode: when the alternate screen buffer is active, mouse wheel events are translated into cursor up/down key presses (or the equivalent application keypad sequences) instead of being reported as mouse events. The sequence is ESC [ ? 1007 h to enable, ESC [ ? 1007 l to disable. This lets users scroll TUI applications that don't natively handle mouse events (like less, man, or vim in default mode) by simply using the mouse wheel — the terminal converts the wheel motion into the same arrow-key sequences a keyboard press would produce. When disabled, mouse wheel events in the alt screen are sent as proper mouse reports instead.
modes.alt-scroll-1007.probe: Send \x1b[?1007h (DECSET 1007), verify the sequence is consumed without breaking subsequent terminal output. Headless backends rarely track this mode, so verification is partial.

modes.utf8-mouse-1005.name: UTF-8 mouse mode (?1005)
modes.utf8-mouse-1005.body: DECSET 1005 enables UTF-8 mouse encoding, an early extension that worked around the 223-column limit of legacy X10 mouse reporting by encoding coordinates as UTF-8 multibyte sequences. The sequence is ESC [ ? 1005 h. Mouse reports use the same format as X10 (ESC [ M + button + col + row) but each coordinate byte may be a multibyte UTF-8 character, supporting columns up to 2015. Mode 1005 sits between the legacy X10 encoding (?1000) and the modern SGR encoding (?1006). It was a transitional standard and is rarely used today — applications and terminals have largely standardized on SGR mouse mode, which avoids the parsing ambiguities of UTF-8 (some bytes can collide with control characters or get corrupted by non-UTF-8-aware middleware). Still implemented by xterm and a few derivatives for backwards compatibility.
modes.utf8-mouse-1005.probe: Send \x1b[?1005h (DECSET 1005), verify the sequence is consumed without breaking subsequent terminal output. Headless backends rarely track this mode, so verification is partial.

modes.deccolm.name: DECCOLM 80/132 column switch (?3)
modes.deccolm.body: DECCOLM (DEC Column Mode) switches the terminal between 80- and 132-column display widths. The sequences are ESC [ ? 3 h for 132 columns and ESC [ ? 3 l for 80 columns. On real VT100 hardware, this changed the dot clock and reprogrammed the CRT to display either 80 or 132 character cells per row, and clearing the screen as a side effect. DECCOLM is a historical VT100/VT220 feature from the era when terminals had fixed character cell sizes and switching column count required physically reprogramming the display. Modern terminal emulators with resizable windows have made it largely obsolete — most ignore DECCOLM unless explicitly enabled (DECSET 40 must be set first to allow column changes). Some emulators like xterm still implement it correctly for compatibility with applications that drive serial terminals or DEC hardware emulators.
modes.deccolm.probe: Send \x1b[?3h (DECCOLM 132), verify the sequence is consumed without breaking subsequent output. Most modern emulators ignore the column change, so verification is partial.

scrollback.accumulate.name: Scrollback accumulates
scrollback.accumulate.body: Tests whether lines scrolled off the top of the visible screen are preserved in the scrollback buffer. This is fundamental terminal behavior — without scrollback accumulation, users cannot scroll up to see previous output. Virtually all modern terminals maintain a scrollback buffer, though the maximum size varies.
scrollback.accumulate.probe: Write 30 lines (exceeding the 24-row screen), verify total line count exceeds 24.

scrollback.total-lines.name: Total line count
scrollback.total-lines.body:
Tests whether the terminal can report the total number of lines in the buffer (visible + scrollback). This is used by testing frameworks and terminal introspection tools to verify scrollback behavior.

Scrollback size is not standardized by ECMA-48, DEC manuals, or terminfo. This probe exists for terminal testing and introspection: a harness needs to know whether output entered scrollback and how much history exists. Real terminal emulators expose this information inconsistently, so Termless treats it as a backend capability rather than a portable app feature.
scrollback.total-lines.probe: Write 30 lines, verify total line count is at least 30.

scrollback.scroll-up.name: Scroll up (SU)
scrollback.scroll-up.body: SU scrolls the content within the scroll region up by N lines, inserting blank lines at the bottom. The sequence is ESC [ N S (default N=1). Lines scrolled off the top enter the scrollback buffer (on the main screen). Used by applications to scroll content without moving the cursor.
scrollback.scroll-up.probe: Write "TOP" on row 1 then fill the screen, send \x1b[S (SU 1), verify row 0 no longer starts with "T".

scrollback.scroll-down.name: Scroll down (SD)
scrollback.scroll-down.body: SD scrolls the content within the scroll region down by N lines, inserting blank lines at the top. The sequence is ESC [ N T (default N=1). Lines scrolled off the bottom margin are lost. The counterpart of SU (scroll up). SD scrolls content down inside the scroll region; it is not the same as moving the viewport through scrollback history. The command is useful for inserting visual space above existing content or implementing smooth-scroll effects. Emulators must discard lines leaving the bottom margin and insert blank lines at the top.
scrollback.scroll-down.probe: Write 3 lines, send \x1b[T (SD 1), verify row 0 is now blank (content shifted down).

scrollback.reverse-index.name: Reverse index (RI)
scrollback.reverse-index.body: RI moves the cursor up one line. If the cursor is at the top margin of the scroll region, the content scrolls down and a blank line is inserted at the top. The sequence is ESC M. This is the reverse of a line feed at the bottom of the scroll region. Used by applications that need to insert content above the current cursor position.
scrollback.reverse-index.probe: Write 3 lines, move to row 0, send \x1bM (RI), verify row 0 is now blank.

scrollback.set-region.name: Scroll region (DECSTBM)
scrollback.set-region.body: DECSTBM (Set Top and Bottom Margins) defines a scroll region — a subset of rows where scrolling operations take effect. The sequence is ESC [ top ; bottom r, where top and bottom are 1-based row numbers. ESC [ r resets to the full screen. Scroll regions are fundamental to TUI applications. A terminal multiplexer like tmux uses them to scroll one pane without affecting others. Editors use them to scroll the document area while keeping status bars fixed. When content scrolls within the region, lines outside the region remain untouched. Virtually every full-screen terminal application depends on DECSTBM. Universally supported since the VT100.
scrollback.set-region.probe: Send \x1b[5;10r (DECSTBM), verify cursor moves to home position (0,0). Then reset with \x1b[r.

scrollback.alt-screen.name: Alt screen separate scrollback
scrollback.alt-screen.body: Tests whether the alternate screen buffer maintains its own separate scrollback, independent from the main screen's scrollback. Most terminals do not provide scrollback in the alternate screen — full-screen applications are expected to manage their own scrolling. Some terminals (like Kitty) offer configurable behavior for alternate screen scrollback.
scrollback.alt-screen.probe: Write "NORMAL", enter alt screen with \x1b[?1049h, verify altScreen mode is active.

scrollback.decstbm.name: DECSTBM constrains scrolling
scrollback.decstbm.body: When a scroll region is set via DECSTBM, scrolling (line feeds, index, SU/SD) should be constrained to the defined region. Lines outside the scroll region must remain fixed. This is the fundamental mechanism that enables fixed headers, footers, and split-screen layouts in TUI applications. Without proper scroll region isolation, tmux panes, vim status bars, and editor chrome would be disrupted by scrolling content.
scrollback.decstbm.probe: Write "FIXED_TOP" on row 0, set DECSTBM 3;10, move inside region, write enough lines to trigger scrolling, verify row 0 still contains "FIXED_TOP".

scrollback.decstbm-reset.name: DECSTBM reset to full screen
scrollback.decstbm-reset.body: DECSTBM with no parameters (ESC [ r) resets the scroll region to the full screen. After reset, scrolling operations should affect all rows. Applications use this to restore normal scrolling behavior after using scroll regions. Failing to properly reset can leave the terminal in a state where only part of the screen scrolls, causing rendering corruption for subsequent applications.
scrollback.decstbm-reset.probe: Set DECSTBM 5;10, then send \x1b[r (no params), write 30 lines, verify full-screen scrolling works (total lines > screen height).

scrollback.viewport-hold-output.name: Viewport holds on output
scrollback.viewport-hold-output.body: Tests whether the visible scrollback viewport stays anchored when new output arrives while the user is scrolled up in the main screen. This is a terminal UI policy, not a VT escape sequence: the application can write output, but the terminal emulator owns the user's viewport position. This behavior matters for long-running inline applications, build logs, and agent UIs. If output forces the viewport back to the live bottom, users lose their place while reading history. If the viewport stays anchored, the newest output remains below the visible region until the user scrolls back down or presses a key that the terminal treats as an explicit return to the live region.
